Title: OpenAI Codex CLI Tutorial | DataCamp
URL: https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/open-ai-codex-cli-tutorial
Published: 2025-04-22T23:00:00.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
In this tutorial, we will learn about OpenAI’s Codex CLI and how to set it up locally. After that, we will use the Codex command to build a website using a screenshot. We will also work on a complex project like training a machine learning model and developing model inference with a custom user interface.
...
OpenAI Codex CLI is an open-source command-line tool that brings the power of OpenAI's latest reasoning models (o4) directly to your terminal. This Lightweight CLI tool lets you read, modify, and execute code directly on your local machine.
...
1. Zero-setup installation: You can get started with a simple NPM command.
2. Multimodal inputs: The CLI allows you to pass text, screenshots, or diagrams, enabling the AI agent to generate or edit code accordingly.
3. Rich approvals workflow: You can choose your level of involvement with three distinct modes: Suggest, Auto Edit, and Full Auto.
4. Runs entirely in your terminal: This feature turns your simple terminal into a code editor, AI assistant, and powerful debugger.
...
In this mode, you can interact with the AI agent conversationally, similar to ChatGPT. Simply type your coding-related queries, and the AI will assist you by generating, debugging, or modifying code as needed.
...
## Building the Website with Codex CLI
...
In this project, we will use Codex CLI to build a website inspired by CodeCut using a screenshot of its design. Why
...
Cut? I’m a big fan of Khuyen Tran’s work, and I wanted to replicate her website’s style while adding my own unique flavor to
...
and prompt you to ask a follow
...
## Building the ML Inference Application
...
In this second project, we will tackle something more complex: building a complete machine learning application. This project will cover everything from data loading to creating a model inference pipeline with a custom user interface.
...
1. Using the command below, we instructed Codex to build the application:
...
```
codex --approval-mode full-auto "Build a proper FastAPI ML application with user interface."
...
As a result, the agent has built a project that includes all the necessary files and configurations. All you need to do is follow the command at the end to run the application.
...
I was particularly impressed by the user interface and how quickly the Codex CLI generated all the files without any bugs.
...
Personally, I find Codex CLI to be a better alternative in many cases. It requires fewer steps to achieve goals, is incredibly fast, and streamlines the development process. However, the only way to determine if this tool will work for your specific use case is to try it yourself. While it excels in straightforward workflows, it may not be the best fit for complex projects with many moving parts.

Title: Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...
URL: https://github.com/openai/codex/blob/main/codex-cli/examples/prompting_guide.md
Published: 2025-04-13T05:37:54.000Z
Author: openai
Highlights:
To see how the Codex CLI works
...
We recently published a[GPT 4.1 prompting guide](https://cookbook.openai.com/examples/gpt4-1_prompting_guide)which contains excellent intuitions for getting the most out of our latest models. It also contains content for how to build agentic workflows from scratch, which may be useful when customizing the Codex CLI for your needs. The Codex CLI is a reference implementation for agentic coding, and puts into practice many of the ideas in that document.
...
There are three common prompting patterns when working with Codex. They roughly traverse task complexity and the level of agency you wish to provide to the Codex CLI.
...
cases where you want Codex to make a minor code change, such as fixing a self-contained bug or adding a small feature, specificity is important. Try to identify the exact change in a way that another human could reflect on your task and verify if their work matches your requirements.
...
the instructions as a file
...
local machine:
...
We recommend putting a sufficient amount of detail that directly states the task in a short and simple description. Add any relevant context that you’d share with someone new to your codebase (if not already in`CODEX.md`). You can also include any files Codex should read for more context, edit or take inspiration from, along with any preferences for how Codex should verify its work
...
Codex can be surprisingly self-sufficient for bigger tasks where your preference might be for the agent to do some heavy lifting up front, and allow you to refine its work later.
...
In such cases where you have a goal in mind but not the exact steps, you can structure your task to give Codex more autonomy to plan, execute and track its progress.
For example:
...
* Add a`.codex/`directory to your working directory. This can act as a shared workspace for you and the agent.
...
* Seed your project directory with a high-level requirements document containing your goals and instructions for how you want it to behave as it executes.
...
* Instruct it to update its plan as it progresses (i.e. "While you work on the project, create dated files such as`.codex/plan\_2025-04-16.md`containing your planned milestones, and update these documents as you progress through the task. For significant pieces of completed work, update the`README.md`with a dated changelog of each functionality introduced and reference the relevant documentation.")
...
### Modes of interaction
...
[](#modes-of-interaction)
For each of these levels of complexity, you can control the degree of autonomy Codex has: let it run in full-auto and audit afterward, or stay in interactive mode and approve each milestone.

Title: Agentic Code Generation with OpenAI Codex CLI — A Knight's Tour Walkthrough - DEV Community
URL: https://dev.to/malcolmlow/agentic-code-generation-with-openai-codex-cli-a-knights-tour-walkthrough-ehj
Published: 2026-05-16T02:51:42.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
A step-by-step walkthrough of using OpenAI's agentic coding tool to scaffold, solve, test, and visualise the classic Knight's Tour chess problem — entirely through natural language prompts.
...
This post assumes you have Codex CLI installed and authenticated. We'll be working in three phases, each driven by a carefully crafted Codex prompt.
...
| Phase | What Codex Builds | Output |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Phase 1 | Core solver using Warnsdorff's heuristic | CLI app, ASCII board output |
| Phase 2 | Pytest test suite and colourised terminal output | ANSI colour board, passing tests |
| Phase 3 | Flask web app with animated HTML canvas | Interactive browser visualisation |
...
in your shell
...
Once the TUI loads, generate an`AGENTS.md` file first. It acts as a standing project-level system prompt — Codex reads it automatically at the start of every future session.
...
## Phase 1 — Core Solver
...
"Create a Python CLI app that solves the Knight's Tour problem. Use Warnsdorff's heuristic: at each step, move to the unvisited square with the fewest onward moves. The board size and starting position (row, col) should be configurable via argparse with defaults of n=8, row=0, col=0. The solver should return None if no tour is found. Display the completed board as a grid of move numbers, right-aligned. Save the solver logic in knight_tour.py and the entry point in main.py. Include a requirements.txt and a .gitignore for Python."
...
## Phase 2 — Tests & Colourised Output
...
"Now add two things. First, create a test suite in tests/test_knight_tour.py using pytest. Test that: (1) solve() returns a valid tour where every integer from 0 to n²−1 appears exactly once, (2) consecutive moves are a valid knight's move apart, (3) solve() returns None for n=2 which has no solution. Second, add a new function print_coloured_board() in knight_tour.py that uses ANSI escape codes to colour the board — alternate between a light and dark background for a chess-style pattern, with white text for the move numbers. Call it from main.py instead of format_board() when --colour flag is passed."
...
## Phase 3 — Flask Web App with Animated Visualisation
...
"Add a Flask web app in app.py. It needs two routes: GET / serves a single-page HTML form where users can input board size (5–10) and starting row/col. POST /solve accepts these inputs, runs the solver, and returns the board as JSON. The HTML page should also contain a JavaScript canvas visualisation that animates the knight's path one move at a time — draw the board as a grid, colour visited squares progressively, and draw a ♞ symbol on the current square. Add flask to requirements.txt."

capable of operating

entire workflows: scaffolding projects,

with terminal commands,

# Beginning the Journey: Launching Codex
...
The process begins
...
The developer installs the Codex CLI and launches it inside a working directory.

Once started, the CLI connects to the`gpt-5.3-codex` model and begins operating within the current workspace.

The Codex sandbox is a controlled execution environment where the Codex agent can safely read files, generate code, and run commands while limiting what it can access on your system. Think of it as a temporary “mini computer” or container that Codex uses to perform coding tasks without risking your machine or infrastructure.

Instead of producing a single snippet of code, Codex analyses the request in the context of the workspace and begins incrementally constructing the project. If it doesn’t find the specified data.csv file, it creates a sample one.

s, running builds, and suggesting improvements such as input debouncing for large datasets.

They participate in the engineering process itself.

The developer remains the architect and decision-maker, but the agent becomes a powerful collaborator capable of analysing repository context, generating structured diffs, coordinating multi-file changes, assisting with debugging and review, and accelerating experimentation.

Title: Codex CLI Workflow: The Setup Senior Devs Use in 2026
URL: https://ofox.ai/blog/codex-cli-real-world-coding-workflow/
Published: 2026-05-11T00:00:00.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
The Codex CLI users who ship the most are not the ones with the cleverest prompts. They are the ones who wrote AGENTS.md once, wired up two MCP servers, and let`/plan` do the thinking on anything ambiguous. Default workflow: plan-first for unclear tasks, just-do-it for clear ones, three to four worktrees in parallel, GPT-5.5 for thinking work and GPT-5.4 Mini for everything boring. This guide is the loop, the trade-offs, and the seven mistakes that eat your first week.
...
1. Open a worktree for the task:`git worktree add ../proj-feat-auth feat/auth`
2. Start Codex in it:`cd ../proj-feat-auth && codex`
3. Decide planning vs. execution: ambiguous →`/plan`, clear → just describe the change
4. Approve diffs with`/permissions` set to Auto for safe ops, Read-only when reviewing
5. Resume tomorrow with`codex resume --last` or`codex resume <SESSION_ID>`
6. Encode anything you correct twice into AGENTS.md or a Skill
...
Plan mode (`/plan` or Shift+Tab) makes Codex gather context and ask clarifying questions before writing code. It is the right choice on ambiguous or multi-step work. It is the wrong choice when you already know what you want.
...
A git worktree is an isolated checkout of the same repo, sharing history but on its own branch and folder. It is how teams run three or four Codex sessions in parallel without thrashing each other’s editor state or build cache.
...
`/goal` is an experimental Codex CLI feature, off by default. You enable it from the`/experimental` picker in the TUI, or by adding`goals = true` under`[features]` in`~/.codex/config.toml`; without that flag, typing`/goal` returns an unknown-command error. Once on, you declare an objective with`/goal `, check status with a bare`/goal`, and control the thread across sessions with`/goal pause`,`/goal resume`, and`/goal clear`. 0.128 introduced the persisted-state plumbing so goals round-trip across`codex resume`; 0.129 made the flag discoverable from inside`/experimental` and changed the lifecycle so paused goals stay paused after`--resume` unless you opt back in. The docs frame it for work where Codex can keep iterating against a verifiable stopping condition without your input for hours at a time.
...
Add MCP servers in`~/.codex/config.toml` or via the Codex App under Settings → MCP servers. The two that almost everyone benefits from on day one: a filesystem server scoped to the repo, and a git server. Anything beyond that, the official best-practices doc is right — add tools only when they unlock a real workflow you already do manually.
...
- 9:00 —`git worktree add ../app-feat-billing feat/billing && cd ../app-feat-billing && codex`
- 9:01 —`/plan`“Add Stripe webhook handling for invoice.paid, idempotent on event_id”

Title: Building Software with AI Agents: A Practical Guide Using OpenAI Codex | by Philippe Haldermans | Medium
URL: https://medium.com/@philippe.haldermans/building-software-with-ai-agents-a-practical-guide-using-openai-codex-15a875c7bb40
Published: 2026-02-16T09:29:40.000Z
Author: Philippe Haldermans
Highlights:
This article lays out a practical workflow for agentic development with OpenAI Codex. It is intentionally concrete, with checklists, real planning examples, and stop conditions you can enforce. The examples use Codex, but the workflow applies to any strong coding agent.
...
This article uses Codex IDE for VS Code, but the workflow applies to any of these agents. The key is not which tool you pick, but how you structure your work to make the agent effective.
...
## Step 1: Prepare
...
Repository for Agent Control
...
## Step 2: Agent Setup with AGENTS.md
...
Many teams rely on chat memory and ad-hoc instructions. This works for small tasks, but breaks down as projects grow. Enter `AGENTS.md`, the most important file in your agentic workflow.
...
`AGENTS.md` is a plain text file (similar to `README.md`) that tells Codex how to work with your codebase. It’s like onboarding documentation, but for your AI teammate.
...
agent can discover
...
test commands without
...
1. Project overview in 2–3 sentences. State what the system does and what it does not do.
2. Exact commands for install/run/test/format/lint. No placeholders.
3. Repo layout + specs so the agent knows where to look for requirements.
4. Domain rules that must never be violated (format of citations, chunk sizes, etc.).
5. Coding conventions, guardrails, and done criteria to prevent drift and define completion.
...
Step 3: Write a Clear Specification
...
A specification defines success criteria in terms Codex can verify. For our Q&A service, we split it into three focused specs: ingestion, retrieval, and Q&A. Each file is small, testable, and versioned.
...
## Step 4: Plan First in Plan Mode
...
This is the flow we just used for the ingestion pipeline:
...
1. Ask for a plan against a specific spec.
2. Let Codex inspect the repo and surface missing decisions.
3. Answer those decisions explicitly.
4. Review and refine the plan until it is decision-complete.
5. Approve, then start implementation.
...
By default, the plan lives in
...
thread. If you want a durable artifact
...
ask Codex to write it to `PLANS.md`. You can edit that file manually and ask Codex to re-plan from it.
...
## Step 5: Execute, Evaluate and Review
...
Once the plan is approved, the remaining work is straightforward: execute, evaluate, then review.
...
Codex runs an implementation loop
...
Codex is not done because the diff looks good. It is done when behavior matches the spec and checks pass.
...
Then run automated checks:
...
- Agent loops on failing tests
...
guardrails in `AGENTS.md
...
- Tests pass but behavior is wrong:
...
too loose. Add behavior-level assertions
